column7: Comments / Context
Direct equivalent. Sums all values of a measure column.
ThoughtSpot uses the full word 'average'; Qlik uses the abbreviation 'Avg'.
Counts total rows. Both include NULLs in count unless DISTINCT is used.
ThoughtSpot uses 'unique count' as a single keyword instead of a DISTINCT modifier.
Direct equivalent. Returns the smallest value in the aggregated set.
Direct equivalent. Returns the largest value in the aggregated set.
Standard deviation. ThoughtSpot spells it 'stddev'; Qlik uses 'Stdev'.
Direct equivalent. Both return the statistical middle value.

column6: Use a workaround with group_count and conditional logic
column7: Qlik returns the most frequent value. ThoughtSpot has no built-in mode() function.

column6: Use if(unique count(col)=1, col, null) as a workaround
column7: Qlik returns a value only if all rows in the group share one value, else NULL. ThoughtSpot has no direct equivalent.
TOTAL in Qlik ignores all chart dimensions to give a grand total. ThoughtSpot achieves this with group_aggregate using empty groupings and filters.
Qlik TOTAL <dim> ignores all dims except the specified one. ThoughtSpot uses group_aggregate with that dim in the groupings list.
Direct equivalent. Returns the statistical variance of a measure column.
ThoughtSpot has a dedicated sum_if(). Qlik nests If() inside Sum() to achieve conditional summing.
ThoughtSpot provides average_if() natively. In Qlik, nest If() inside Avg().
ThoughtSpot has a built-in count_if(). Qlik achieves this by nesting If() inside Count().
ThoughtSpot has unique_count_if(). Qlik uses Count(DISTINCT If(...)).
ThoughtSpot has stddev_if() for conditional standard deviation. Qlik nests If() inside Stdev().
ThoughtSpot has variance_if() natively. Qlik nests If() inside Variance().

column5: if condition then val1 else val2
column7: ThoughtSpot uses keyword syntax (if/then/else). Qlik uses a function call If(). Both support chained else-if.

column5: if c1 then v1 else if c2 then v2 else v3
column6: if revenue>100000 then 'Platinum' else if revenue>50000 then 'Gold' else 'Silver'
column7: Nested conditionals. ThoughtSpot uses chained else-if keywords; Qlik nests If() calls.
ThoughtSpot has a dedicated ifnull() for null replacement in one call. Qlik requires wrapping IsNull() inside If().
Direct equivalent. Both return true/1 when the value is null.
Direct equivalent. Both check for non-null values using a not prefix.
Qlik Match() returns position of match (1-based). ThoughtSpot uses the 'in' operator for membership testing — simpler and more readable.
ThoughtSpot uses 'not in' operator directly. Qlik uses Not around Match().
Qlik Mixmatch is case-insensitive Match. ThoughtSpot uses lower() on both sides to simulate.
Qlik supports wildcard patterns with *. ThoughtSpot uses contains() for substring checks — no wildcard syntax in formulas.

column5: if n=1 then val1 else if n=2 then val2 ...
column6: if tier=1 then 'Low' else if tier=2 then 'Mid' else 'High'
column7: Qlik Pick() selects the nth value from a list. ThoughtSpot has no Pick() — replicate with chained if/then/else.
Direct equivalent. Boolean AND operator — both conditions must be true.
Direct equivalent. Boolean OR operator — at least one condition must be true.
Direct equivalent. Negates a boolean condition.
Qlik {1} ignores ALL user selections (grand total). ThoughtSpot group_aggregate with empty groupings/filters achieves the same result.
Qlik {$} is the current selection — the default. ThoughtSpot always uses the current filter context by default.
Both allow hardcoded filter overrides. Qlik uses Set Expression {}. ThoughtSpot uses group_aggregate's third filter argument.
Qlik -= removes a value from the current selection set. ThoughtSpot has no exact -= syntax but query_filters() can be combined to control filter scope.
Qlik += adds a value to the current selection. ThoughtSpot uses query_groups() + {attr} to extend grouping scope.
Qlik {$<>} applies an override on top of current selections. ThoughtSpot uses group_aggregate with query_groups() to inherit dims and a custom filter.
ThoughtSpot-specific. query_groups() and query_filters() inherit the current search's dimensions and filters. No direct Qlik equivalent.
Both compute aggregation at a different grain. Qlik creates a virtual table with Aggr(); ThoughtSpot uses group_sum() directly.
Qlik uses Aggr() wrapper around Avg(). ThoughtSpot has a dedicated group_average() function.
Qlik uses Aggr() wrapper around Count(). ThoughtSpot has a dedicated group_count() function.
Qlik uses Aggr() wrapper around Max(). ThoughtSpot has a dedicated group_max() function.
Qlik uses Aggr() wrapper around Min(). ThoughtSpot has a dedicated group_min() function.
Qlik uses Aggr() around Stdev(). ThoughtSpot has group_stddev() as a direct function.
ThoughtSpot has group_unique_count() natively. Qlik wraps Count(DISTINCT ...) inside Aggr().
Nested aggregation — find max across customer totals. ThoughtSpot supports direct nesting; Qlik wraps Aggr() inside outer aggregation.
Average orders per customer. ThoughtSpot nests group_count() inside average(); Qlik wraps Aggr() inside Avg().
Running total. ThoughtSpot has dedicated cumulative_sum(). Qlik combines RangeSum() + Above().
Running average. ThoughtSpot provides cumulative_average() natively. Qlik builds it with RangeAvg() + Above().
Running maximum. ThoughtSpot has cumulative_max() natively. Qlik uses RangeMax() + Above().
Running minimum. ThoughtSpot has cumulative_min() natively. Qlik uses RangeMin() + Above().

column7: Qlik returns value from the top (first) row of the current column segment. No direct ThoughtSpot equivalent.

Qlik pivot-only — returns value from next column. No ThoughtSpot equivalent.

column7: Qlik returns value of the nth measure column in a straight table. ThoughtSpot has no column-index reference — use named columns.

Qlik returns total row count of current chart segment. ThoughtSpot's count() on a key column approximates total row count.

column7: Qlik script function that looks up a previously loaded row's value. ThoughtSpot formulas operate on already-loaded data — handle row-lookups in ETL.

column7: Qlik script VLOOKUP across tables. ThoughtSpot handles multi-table lookups through data model/worksheet joins — not in formula syntax.

column6: Handle sequential row logic in ETL before loading
column7: Qlik script function returning previous row's value during data load. ThoughtSpot has no sequential row reference in formula context.

column6: Handle in data model — use JOINs or filters
column7: Qlik script checks if a value exists in a previously loaded field. In ThoughtSpot, use data model joins or worksheet-level filters.
Qlik script maps values using a mapping table. ThoughtSpot replicates with if/then/else chains or pre-maps in the data pipeline.
Qlik replaces substrings using a mapping table. ThoughtSpot's replace() substitutes fixed values; for complex mappings use ETL.

column6: No direct equivalent in ThoughtSpot formulas
column7: Qlik returns the nth distinct value of a field. ThoughtSpot has no field-enumeration function in formula context.

Direct equivalent. Both extract the 4-digit year from a date column.
Qlik returns month name (e.g. 'Jan'). ThoughtSpot returns numeric month (1-12) by default.
Direct equivalent. Both extract the day of month (1-31).
Direct equivalent. Both return quarter number (1-4).
Both return ISO week number. Naming differs: Qlik uses Week(); ThoughtSpot uses week_number_of_year().
Qlik returns a number (0=Mon, 6=Sun). ThoughtSpot returns the day name (e.g. 'Monday').
Qlik returns the year the ISO week belongs to (can differ near year boundaries). ThoughtSpot year() returns calendar year — may differ for week 53.
Direct equivalent. Both extract the hour (0-23) from a datetime column.
Direct equivalent. Both extract the minute (0-59).
Direct equivalent. Both extract seconds (0-59).
Direct equivalent. Both return today's date with no time component.
Direct equivalent. Both return the current date and time as a timestamp.
Both return the first day of the month. Different naming but identical behavior.
Qlik has built-in MonthEnd(). ThoughtSpot has no end-of-month function — workaround: start of next month minus 1 day.
Direct equivalent behavior. Naming convention differs.
Qlik has QuarterEnd(). ThoughtSpot has no equivalent — workaround: start of next quarter minus 1 day.
Direct equivalent behavior. Different naming convention only.
Qlik has YearEnd(). ThoughtSpot has no equivalent — workaround: start of next year minus 1 day.
Both return the start of the week. Naming differs; behavior is equivalent.
Qlik has WeekEnd(). ThoughtSpot workaround: add 6 days to start_of_week().
Direct equivalent. Both add or subtract months. Use negative n to go backward.
ThoughtSpot has a native add_years(). No add_months(date,12) workaround needed.
Qlik Age() returns exact age in years. ThoughtSpot approximates age via date subtraction divided by 365.
Qlik NetworkDays() excludes weekends and optionally holidays. ThoughtSpot date_diff() returns total calendar days only — no business day logic.
Both convert a value to a date. Format strings differ: Qlik uses 'YYYY-MM-DD'; ThoughtSpot uses strftime '%Y-%m-%d'.
Qlik Timestamp() formats a datetime as a string. ThoughtSpot uses to_date() for parsing strings into dates.
Qlik DayStart() returns midnight of the given date. ThoughtSpot date_trunc to 'day' is the equivalent.
Qlik has dedicated MakeDate() to construct a date from parts. ThoughtSpot workaround uses concat + to_date.
Qlik InYear() checks if a date falls in a relative year. ThoughtSpot replicates by comparing year() extractions.
Qlik InMonth() checks if a date is in the same month as the base. ThoughtSpot matches both year and month components.
Qlik InWeek() checks if a date is in the same week as the base. ThoughtSpot compares start_of_week() values.
Qlik InQuarter() checks if a date falls in the same quarter. ThoughtSpot compares start_of_quarter() values.
Qlik InYearToDate() checks if a date falls within the YTD window. ThoughtSpot uses a range comparison from start_of_year to today.
Qlik computes date difference by simple subtraction. ThoughtSpot uses the explicit date_diff() function. Both return days.

ThoughtSpot has no native lower() in formula context; use sql_string_op to push LOWER to the warehouse (matches the Tableau converter).

ThoughtSpot-only. query_groups() inherits the current search's dimension groupings dynamically inside group_aggregate(). Adapts as users change their search.
ThoughtSpot-only. query_filters() inherits the current search's applied filters inside group_aggregate(). No Qlik formula equivalent.
ThoughtSpot-only. Extends current search groupings by adding an extra dimension to scope. No direct Qlik equivalent.
